segments.s468-g161: گرفتند is past simple, 3rd-person plural (they took/they uttered); here it participates in the idiom گرفتن آفرین meaning “to give/utter praise.” یکسر is an adverb modifying the whole action meaning “all at once/unanimously.” برو آفرین is the exclamation they uttered — برو functions as an intensifying/interjective element attached to the acclamation آفرین (the shout “Bravo!”). Word order is poetic (verb before adverb and then the quoted exclamation).
segments.s468-g162: بدان is the prepositional phrase “to that” (بد- = به “to/for” + an old/demonstrative element); in older poetic Persian the components may appear fused or slightly altered. سرور شهریار زمین is a clustered titulary: سرور (“lord”) + شهریار (“king/sovereign”) with زمین (“land, country”) functioning as the domain (equivalent to “king of the land”); the usual ezāfe linking is omitted in poetry, so read it as apposition/possessive sequence: “that lord, the sovereign of the land.”
segments.s468-g137: This clause ends with the past-tense verb نهاد (third-person singular) meaning 'placed/put', with no explicit subject — a common Persian narrative economy where the subject is understood from context. به + درون marks direction/position 'into/inside', so the phrase literally reads 'placed (it) the golden throne inside the tomb'. Word order is essentially object + location + verb (a typical Persian order in poetry).
segments.s468-g138: یکی is an indefinite subject 'someone/a certain one'. بر سرش is a prepositional phrase 'on his head' where -ش is the enclitic third-person possessive 'his'. مشکین here describes the تاج and is best read as 'dark/black'. The verb نهاد again is past 'placed/put', so the clause means 'someone placed a dark crown on his head.'
segments.s468-g139: نهادش is the past verb نهاد plus the enclitic object -ش 'him/it', giving 'they placed/laid him'. به + تابوت زر اندرون is a directional phrase 'into the gold-coffin/into the golden coffin' (زر qualifies تابوت). The clause reads 'they put him into the golden coffin.'
segments.s468-g140: بروبر is an adverb meaning 'directly/straight/just so' modifying the verb ببارید (past 'fell' for drops). ز is the poetic preposition 'from', مژگان 'eyelashes', and خون 'blood'. The clause literally says 'blood fell directly from the eyelashes' (i.e., blood streamed from his lashes).
segments.s468-g141: چو here means 'when'. تابوتش is 'his coffin' (تابوت + possessive -ش). از جای uses از 'from' plus جای 'place/spot' to indicate removal from its place. برداشتند is past plural 'they lifted/removed'. The clause = 'When they lifted his coffin from the place...'
segments.s468-g142: همه 'everyone/all' is subject; دست بر دست is an idiomatic repeated-noun prepositional phrase literally 'hand upon hand'; بگذاشتند is past plural 'they placed/put'. Together it describes a collective gesture — literally 'everyone put hand upon hand' (i.e., folded/placed their hands).
segments.s468-g143: سکندر is the proper name 'Alexander'. پیاده following the name means 'on foot' (a descriptive postpositive adjective). به پیش اندرون is a directional cluster: به پیش = 'forward/toward (the presence of)' and اندرون = 'the inner part/inner chamber', so the clause says 'Alexander, on foot, went forward into the inner chamber.'
segments.s468-g144: بزرگان همه = 'all the great ones/the nobles' (بزرگان = great men). دیدگان is the plural 'eyes/visions'; پر ز is the adjectival phrase 'full of' (پر + ز), and خون is 'blood'. The clause means 'all the nobles' eyes were full of blood.'
segments.s468-g121: کف دست او بر دهان برنهاد is a noun phrase followed by a past-tense verb: کف دست او (‘his palm’) + بر دهان (‘on the mouth’) + برنهاد (past, ‘placed/laid upon’). Persian here uses the expected sequence of possessor phrase + locative phrase followed by the verb; برنهاد is an older/poetic past form of برنهادن meaning ‘to place upon’.
segments.s468-g122: بدو گفت یزدان پناه تو باد: بدو (‘to him’) + گفت (here contextually a speech-verb ‘said’, though the provided senses list lacks that verbal sense) + a short optative formula یزدان پناه تو باد. یزدان پناه تو باد literally ‘God [be] refuge for you’ where باد is the optative particle ‘may’. There is no copula; the construction is a fixed prayer/blessing.
segments.s468-g123: سپردم ترا جای و رفتم به خاک: سپردم is 1st-person past ‘I entrusted/left’, ترا is the object ‘you’ (here written as ت + را: second-person suffix + object marker), جای is the complement ‘place/position’. The clause is followed by و رفتم به خاک (‘and I went to the earth’ = ‘I went to the dust/was buried’). Word order is verb + object/complement (poetic inversion is possible), and ترا shows the enclitic object marking.
segments.s468-g124: سپردم روانرا به یزدان پاک repeats the verb سپردم (‘I entrust(ed)’) with روانرا (‘the soul’ as definite object – روان + را) and a prepositional goal به یزدان پاک (‘to the pure God’). The verb is past 1st-person and takes روانرا as its object; یزدان پاک is the recipient introduced by به.
segments.s468-g125: بگفت این و جانش برآمد ز تن: بگفت is a past-tense third-person ‘he said’ (بَ‑گفت, narrative past), این ‘this’ is the direct object of گفت, and then و joins the next clause: جانش (‘his life’, جان + possessive suffix ش) برآمد (‘arose/issued forth’, past) ز تن (‘from the body’). The possessive suffix is attached to جان, and the verb برآمد expresses the soul leaving the body.
segments.s468-g126: برو زار بگریستند انجمن: بگریستند is the past-plural verb ‘they wept’; انجمن (‘the assembly’) appears after the verb by poetic inversion and is the subject. بُرو زار is an adverbial/parenthetical phrase indicating the manner — ‘lamentingly/bitterly’ — modifying بگریستند. So the clause means ‘the assembly wailed and wept.’
segments.s468-g127: سکندر همه جامه‌ها کرد چاک: subject سکندر ('Alexander') + object همه جامه‌ها (‘all the garments’, plural) + کرد (past ‘did/made’) + چاک (resultative/adjective ‘torn’). The construction کرد + adjective/participle functions causatively/resultatively: ‘made the garments torn’ = ‘tore his garments.’ Plural marking -ها on جامه‌ها indicates multiple garments.
segments.s468-g128: به تاج کیان بر پراگند خاک: به تاج کیان is a prepositional phrase ‘upon the crowns of the Kayanids’ (تاج governing the genitive relation with کیان). بر پراگند خاک functions as a verbal/adverbial cluster meaning ‘scattered dust upon’—پراگند as a participial/adjectival element ‘scattered’ modifying خاک (‘dust/earth’), with بر indicating the action of strewing/over. Together they convey ‘scattered dust upon the crowns of the kings.’
segments.s468-g153: دو بدخواه را is the direct object phrase ('two ill‑wishers' + Persian object marker را). زنده modifies بردار (literally 'take alive'). بردار کرد is a poetic/periphrastic past: بردار (take) + کرد ('made/did') yields 'had/took (them) alive' — i.e. caused them to be seized alive rather than killed.
segments.s468-g154: سر شاه‌کش is a noun phrase 'the head of the king‑slayer' (modifier after the head is understood in classical style). مرد بیدار کرد is a second clause: مرد 'the man' + بیدار کرد 'caused to wake / roused' — کرد is the past/cause verb. The two clauses are paratactic (no conjunction): he did X; he did Y.
segments.s468-g155: ز (poetic) = 'from'; لشکر = 'the host/army'. برفتند is the simple past 'they went/advanced'. In classical/poetic order the verb comes before the subject: 'from the host advanced the men of battle' (مردان جنگ = 'warriors' / 'men of battle').
segments.s468-g156: گرفته is a perfect/participle 'having seized' (or 'seized'), modifying the following nouns. یکی سنگ literally 'one (a man) a stone seized' — i.e. 'one took a stone' — and the trailing phrase هر یک به چنگ 'each one with it in his grasp' (به + چنگ = 'in/into the grip' — چنگ here = 'claw/hand, i.e. grasp'). This is compressed, typical epic word order.
segments.s468-g157: بکردند is 'they did/caused' (past). بر دارشان is a locative phrase 'upon their دار' where دار here means 'gallows'; سنگسار is the nominalized punishment 'stoning'. Together: 'they caused stoning upon their gallows' = 'they had them stoned on the gallows.'
segments.s468-g158: مبادا introduces a negative purpose/fear 'lest / may it not be that...'. کسی 'anyone' is the indefinite subject. کو here is an archaic particle appearing in classical Persian (contextually linking the clause), and کشد is the present/subjunctive form of 'to kill' used after مبادا ('lest anyone kill'). شهریار is the object 'the king'.
segments.s468-g159: چو = 'when'; دیدند = past 'they saw'. ایرانیان is the subject 'the Iranians' (placed after the verb in poetic order). کو here is an archaic linking particle; چه کرد = 'what he had done' (چه = what, کرد = did). Together: 'When the Iranians saw what he had done...'.
segments.s468-g160: بزاری is a derogatory label 'the wretch' or 'the miserable one' (here meaning 'that miserable man'). بران = 'to/on that' (pointing to the following noun), شاه = 'king', آزادمرد is an epithet 'noble/liberal man' describing the king. The phrase means 'what the wretch had done to that noble king.'
segments.s468-g129: یکی is an indefinite subject meaning 'someone' or 'one'; دخمه is a noun 'tomb/sepulchre'. کردش is the past-tense verb کرد plus the enclitic object -ش 'him' (so 'made/placed him'); بر آیین او is a prepositional phrase 'according to his custom/rite' that follows the verb. Word order is typical classical Persian: subject (indefinite) → verb (+ object clitic) → prepositional phrase.
segments.s468-g130: بدان سان که is a frozen adverbial phrase 'in such a manner that / so that' (here segmented into ب + دان + سان + که); it introduces the manner clause that follows. بد فره و دین او functions as a goal/concern phrase 'for his fravash/divine glory and his religion' (فره = divine glory, دین = faith). The exact morphology shows classical/poetic compression of prepositions and particles, so some pieces appear as bound forms.
segments.s468-g131: بشستن is the infinitive/verbal-noun 'washing' (used here as the verbal action 'they washed'); ازان is a genitive/adverbial 'of that' (i.e. that blood); خون is the direct object 'blood'; به روشن گلاب uses به + adjective + noun to indicate instrument or means ('with/by bright/clear rosewater') — here به is instrumental/associative ('with').
segments.s468-g132: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; آمدش is آمد + enclitic -ش 'came to him' (past tense, 3rd sing. with dative clitic); هنگام جاوید خواب is a temporal noun phrase 'the time of eternal sleep' with هنگام 'at the time of' governing the phrase and جاوید modifying خواب ('eternal sleep').
segments.s468-g133: بیاراستندش is the past plural verb بی‌آراستند 'they adorned' with the object-clitic -ش 'him'; به introduces the material or garment ('in / with'); دیبای روم is a classical genitive-like compound 'Roman brocade' (classical Persian often drops the ezâfe in poetry, so 'دیبای روم' = 'the brocade of Rome').
segments.s468-g134: همه پیکرش is 'his whole/entire body' (همه = whole/entire, پیکرش = پیکر + -ش 'his body'); گوهر و زر بوم is a condensed noun cluster meaning his body 'was (a mass) of gems and gold of the land' — poetically condensed: گوهر و زر enumerate the precious materials covering the body, and بوم (here) is a poetic locution/qualifier (literally 'of the land/realm'). The copula ('was') is omitted, as is common in Persian poetry.
segments.s468-g135: تنش is تن + enclitic -ش 'his body'; زیر کافور is a locative/covering phrase 'under camphor' (زیر = under, کافور = camphor); شد is the past-tense auxiliary/verb 'became' and ناپدید is a predicate adjective 'invisible/vanished' — together 'his body became vanished/hidden under camphor.'
segments.s468-g136: ازان پس is a temporal adverbial 'from that time on / thereafter'; کسی ... ندید is a negative existential clause 'no one saw' (کسی here functions in the sense 'no one' because of the negative verb ندید); روی دارا = 'the face of Dârâ' (روی = face, دارا here is the personal name Dârâ in context). The verb ندید is past tense 'did not see.'
segments.s468-g81: به اندرز من is a prepositional phrase meaning 'to my counsel' (i.e., 'heed my advice'). سر به سر is a reduplicated phrase functioning adverbially here — literally 'head to head' but idiomatically 'completely / with full attention'. گوش دار is an imperative construction literally 'have ear' (i.e., 'listen, pay attention'); دار here functions as an archaic/poetic imperative ('have/hold') rather than the noun senses given in the list.
segments.s468-g82: پذیرنده باش is a straightforward imperative 'be receptive' (پذیرنده = receptive; باش = be!). و links this command to the next clause. بدل is an adverbial 'in return / in exchange', and هوش دار is another imperative phrase 'have awareness / keep your wits' (again with دار as an archaic/poetic imperative). The two imperatives form a parallel pair (be receptive / be alert).
segments.s468-g83: Typical narrative word order: subject (سکندر) + indirect object (بدو = 'to him') + past verb گفت ('said'). فرمان تراست is the quoted/predicative content: فرمان = 'command' and تراست is the fused older form meaning 'it is yours' (تـَ + است), indicating possession."
segments.s468-g84: بگو is the imperative 'say'. آنچ functions as a relative/indefinite 'that which / whatever'. خواهی here is the 2nd-person present/optative sense 'you wish/desire' used inside the relative clause (i.e., 'whatever you wish'). که introduces the clause relating آنچ to the following nominative phrase; پیمان تراست is a nominal clause 'the pact/covenant is yours' with تراست again meaning 'it belongs to you.'
segments.s468-g85: زبان تیز is a descriptive noun phrase 'sharp-tongued' where زبان (here = 'speech/utterance') is qualified by the adjective تیز. دارا is a proper name in context (Dara) and stands in apposition; بدو is the indirect object 'to him'; برگشاد is a past/aorist verb 'opened' (i.e., 'opened his speech' or 'began to speak'). Word order places the descriptive phrase before the verb for poetic effect.
segments.s468-g86: همی کرد is a classical imperfective/past habitual construction ('he began/was doing' or 'he would do'), where همی marks continuous aspect and کرد is the auxiliary/main verb 'did/made'. سرتاسر = 'throughout' modifies اندرز یاد, a compound nominal phrase meaning 'remembrance/recitation of counsel' (i.e., 'the whole counsel'). The clause as a whole means he proceeded to recite the entire counsel.
segments.s468-g87: نخستین here adverbially 'first' (introducing speech). چنین گفت is the common narrative formula 'thus he spoke.' کای is a vocative particle 'O' addressing someone directly (not the title Kay here), and نامدار is the vocative epithet 'renowned one.' The phrase functions as the opening of direct address.
segments.s468-g88: بترس is an imperative 'be afraid / fear!' with از introducing the object of fear. جهان داور is 'judge of the world' (جهان = world; داور = judge), and کردگار is an appositive specifying the divine Creator — together 'the world's Judge, the Creator.' The preposition از governs the whole phrase as the thing to be feared.
segments.s468-g145: چنین is an adverb meaning “thus/in this way.” تا here is the subordinating conjunction “until,” introducing a temporal clause. ستودان is a verbal noun (masdar) meaning “praise(s)” and functions as the event/topic that persists until the point named; دارا is the name Dārā (here the king) and modifies/possesses the praise. برفت is an archaic past tense verb (3rd sg.) meaning “went away/departed/ceased” (classical compound of بر + رفتن), marking the end of that state.
segments.s468-g146: همی is the continuous/adverbial particle indicating repeated or ongoing action (“kept/continually”). پوست is the noun “skin/hide” (here a lexical item being uttered or referenced as a word). گفتی is a past‑tense reportive form meaning “said” (classical/poetic past) and links to همی to show repeated utterance. بروبر is an adverbial phrase meaning “directly/face to face/exactly.” بکفت is a past impersonal/past‑result form meaning “it sufficed/was enough,” concluding the clause.
segments.s468-g147: چو is the temporal conjunction “when.” بر تخت is a prepositional phrase meaning “on/upon the throne” (بر + تخت). بنهاد is a classical past tense verb (3rd sg.) meaning “placed/laid (on)” (بنهادن = to place), taking تابوت شاه as its object. تابوت شاه is a genitival phrase “the king’s coffin.”
segments.s468-g148: بر آیین شاهان is an idiomatic prepositional phrase “according to the ritual/custom of kings” (بر = according to; آیین = ritual/custom; شاهان = kings). برآورد is a past tense verb here meaning “they brought/led/carried (it) forth” or more loosely “they set forth”; راه is the overt noun “road/route” governed by that verb. Together the clause says they proceeded along the road following royal custom.
segments.s468-g149: چو introduces a temporal clause (“when”). پردخت is a noun derived from پرداختن and here means something like “the adorned/finished (thing or procession)”—it names what emerges. از دخمه is the prepositional phrase “from the tomb” (دخمه = sepulchre/vault). ارجمند is an adjective “esteemed/precious” qualifying the tomb (or the thing associated with it).
segments.s468-g150: ز (poetic form of از) + بیرون = “from/outside.” بزد is the past tense verb “he/they struck/smote/sounded” (classical past). دارهای بلند is a noun phrase “tall poles/stands” (دار in classical usage can mean a pole/tree/stand or gallows) with the adjective بلند “tall”; grammatically it is the object or the thing affected by the verb بزد (i.e., poles were struck/raised/sounded outside).
segments.s468-g151: یکی is the indefinite numeral “one” / “a certain.” دار here names a single pole/stand/tree (classical دار = pole/stand). بر نام functions as “bearing the name” or “with the name” (بر + نام), and جانوشیار is a proper name (the name borne by that pole). The phrase describes individual named poles.
segments.s468-g152: دگر is “another/other.” همچنان is an adverb “likewise/in the same manner” linking this item to the preceding one. از در means “from the gate/door” (از = from; در here = gate/entrance). ماهیار is a proper name (Māhyār) specifying whose gate/place is meant. The whole small clause says there was another similar pole at Māhyār’s gate.
segments.s468-g65: ز here is the poetic form of از meaning 'from/among'; «ز خویشان» = 'among (my) relatives'. «کسی نیست» is the existential negative construction 'there is no one' (کسی + نیست). The noun فریادرس follows this clause as the predicate (there is no one who is a rescuer), a common Persian order where the existential 'کسی نیست' is followed by the role/quality.
segments.s468-g66: «امیدم» is امید + the 1st-person suffix -م 'my hope'. «به» is the preposition 'in/to/with' marking the target of the hope. «پروردگارست» is the fused poetic form of «پروردگار است» (the copula is attached), meaning 'is in/with the Lord' here. «و بس» is a short concluding phrase 'and that's enough/only' (i.e. 'only that').
segments.s468-g67: «برین گونه» is the poetic contraction of «بر این گونه» meaning 'in this manner/thus' (بر + این + گونه). «خسته» is a predicative adjective 'weary/exhausted'. «خاک اندرَم» has اندر + the 1st-person suffix -م (اندرم) and means 'I am in/on the earth' or 'I lie on the ground'; the verb 'to be' is implicit in this classical phrasing.
segments.s468-g68: «ز گیتی» = 'from the world' (ز = from, گیتی = the world). «به دام هلاک» is a prepositional phrase 'into the snare/trap of destruction' where دام = 'trap' and هلاک = 'ruin/destruction'. Again «اندرم» carries the 1st-person suffix ('I am in/ensnared'), so the whole clause says the speaker is caught by doom from the world.
segments.s468-g69: «چنین است» = 'such is' or 'thus it is'. «آیینِ چرخِ روان» is a genitive chain: آیین = 'custom/law/order', چرخ = 'wheel' used figuratively for fate/cycle, and روان here is adjectival 'moving/flowing' (the turning wheel). The phrase means 'the custom/order of the moving (i.e. revolving) wheel' — i.e. the operation of fate.
segments.s468-g70: This is a two-part conditional/contrast: «اگر شهریارم» = 'if I am a king' (شهریار + 1st-person suffix -م) and «و‌گر پهلوان» = 'or if (I am) a champion/hero' (وگر is the poetic 'or if'). The copula 'am' is expressed by the suffix in شهریارم; no separate verb is needed.
segments.s468-g71: «بزرگی» is the noun 'greatness'. «به فرجام» is a prepositional phrase 'to the end' or 'in the end'. «هم بگذرد» uses هم 'also/too' plus the classical verb بگذرد (3rd-person singular) meaning 'passes' or 'will pass away'; the line states that even greatness comes to an end.
segments.s468-g72: «شکارست» is the archaic fused form of «شکار است» meaning 'is prey/is destined to be taken'. «مرگش» = 'its/his death' (مرگ + third-person suffix -ش). «همی» is an emphatic/adverbial particle ('indeed/just/surely') and «بشکرد» is an archaic verb here meaning 'comes to pass' or 'befalls'. Together: 'death will surely befall it/him.'
segments.s468-g49: بد و نیک هر دو is a coordinated nominal phrase meaning “both the bad and the good.” هر دو emphasises the duality. ز is the poetic form of از meaning “from,” and یزدان‌شناس is a compound noun meaning “one who knows God / a pious person,” so the phrase reads “both bad and good (are) from the God‑knower.”
segments.s468-g50: وزو (an older spelling of وضو) = ritual ablution; دار here functions as an archaic verbal/imperative‑like form meaning “have/keep” or “perform” (i.e. ‘keep up ablution’). تا زنده باشی is a temporal clause “as long as you live” (تا = until/as long as; باشی = subjunctive/present form of بودن used after تا). سپاس is a nominal imperative/adjunct meaning “thanks / be grateful / praise.”
segments.s468-g51: نمودار here is a nominal predicate meaning “manifest sign / mark.” گفتار governs it as a genitive: “the mark of my speech.” من‌من is a noun meaning “murmuring / hesitating whisper,” and بسم is the archaic form of بس meaning “enough” — so the clause reads roughly “the sign of my words: ‘my murmuring is enough.’” This is a nominal sentence (no overt copula).
segments.s468-g52: بدین here is the classical prepositional phrase meaning “by this / with this” (ب + دین used idiomatically), در = “in” and نکوهیده هرکسم = “anyone who is censured / blamed.” Together the segment means “by this, anyone who is censured (in religion / this matter).” Note the older word order and the elliptical nature: the full sense depends on the previous clause.
segments.s468-g53: که introduces a clause (“for/that”). چندان is an intensifier “so much / such,” and the three nouns بزرگی, شاهی, گنج are coordinated objects: “greatness, kingship, and treasure.” The phrase lists the high goods being referred to; the verb is supplied by the following segment (ellipsis across clauses).
segments.s468-g54: نبد is an archaic negative past existential (≈ نبود) meaning “there was not / did not exist.” در زمانه کس = “in that age any person” (در زمانه = in the age; کس = anyone/someone). از من marks source “from/of me,” and به رنج indicates instrument/means “by/with hardship.” Syntactically this makes a negative existential: “there was no one in the age who (had) such … from me without hardship.”
segments.s468-g55: همان نیز = “that likewise / the same also,” linking back to what was just mentioned. چندان is “so much / so many,” followed by a coordinated nominal group سلیح و سپاه = “weapons and army/host.” The verb (existence/possession) is again implied from the earlier clause (ellipsis of نبد/نبود).
segments.s468-g56: گرانمایه is an adjectival qualifier “valuable / precious” that applies to the following nouns. اسپان = horses (often cavalry), and تخت و کلاه are an additional coordinated pair “throne and helmet,” completing the list of precious military and regal possessions. Word order is adjective + noun, typical in older Persian poetic diction.
segments.s468-g97: نیاری is used as a vocative here ('O companion, O friend'), an address to someone; به فرزند من marks the target/recipient with the preposition به ('to/at') and من is the possessor 'my'; سرزنش is a noun 'reproach' (a verbal-noun) that names what should not be directed at the son. Note that the negative that forbids the action appears in the next segment (نه) so this segment gives the thing being prohibited and its target.
segments.s468-g98: نه is the clause negator ('do not' / 'nor'); پیغاره is a noun meaning 'message/word/rumor' used here for 'topic of talk' or 'subject of report'; از introduces the agent/source ('by/from the people'); مردم is 'the people'; بدکنش is an adjectival/nominal compound meaning 'of bad conduct' or 'ill‑doers' describing those people. Together the clause forbids making the son the target of the people's bad talk.
segments.s468-g99: چو is the temporal/clausal introducer 'when/as'; پرورده is a past-participle used predicatively 'reared/nurtured'; شهریاران (plural) names the agents ('by the kings/lords') without an explicit preposition in poetic style; بود is the past copula 'was'. The phrase thus means 'when he had been reared by kings'.
segments.s468-g100: به introduces a locative/scene-marking phrase here ('at/in the banquet'); بزم is 'banquet/assembly'; افسر in poetry often means a diadem/ornament (literal senses listed include 'helmet') and functions as the predicate noun 'the ornament/diadem'; نامداران is a genitive/attributive plural 'of the renowned/illustrious'; بود is again the copula 'was.' The whole clause says he was the ornament/diadem at the illustrious feast.
segments.s468-g101: مگر here introduces a rhetorical/exclusive question ('is there not / except'); ز (written before و as زو) is the poetic contraction of از او 'from him'; ببینی is the verb 'you see' (ب‑ prefix marks classical present/subjunctive/optative forms in Persian poetry); یکی is 'one / a single (person)'; نامدار is an adjective 'renowned' modifying یکی. The clause asks rhetorically whether one famed person can be seen from him.
segments.s468-g102: کجا is the interrogative 'where'; نو is the adjective 'new' used with کند to mean 'make new/renew'; کند is the verb 'does/makes' (present/subjunctive form); نام is the direct object 'name/fame'; اسفندیار is the proper name whose name/fame is meant. The clause asks where Esfandiyar would renew or make his name anew — i.e. where he would win fresh fame.
segments.s468-g103: بیاراید is a present/subjunctive/optative verb meaning 'adorns/arrays' (ب‑ prefix is the classical verbal prefix); این آتش is 'this fire' (demonstrative + noun); زردهشت is the poetic form of زردشت (Zarathushtra/Zoroaster) and here qualifies the fire as Zoroastrian or names the prophet associated with the fire. The word-order is typical of classical poetry (noun phrases juxtaposed without an ezāfe).
segments.s468-g104: بگیرد is a b‑prefixed present/subjunctive form of گرفت 'take/seize' ('it seizes' / 'it holds'); همان is a demonstrative 'that same/that very'; زند and استا appear as two coordinated words (زند is an archaic/poetic form related to 'زندگی' or a verbal root, and استا is a verb 'stand/stop') joined by و 'and'; بمشت is a compact poetic phrase literally 'in the (his) fist/hand' (here used image‑wise). Taken together, the clause says that the same (fire) seizes/grasps and stands firm in the hand — an idiomatic, poetic way to say it takes hold and stands fast. (Note: these words appear in archaic/poetic forms and some readings are contracted for meter.)
segments.s468-g89: 'که' begins a relative clause meaning 'who/that'. The list 'چرخ و زمین و زمان' is a coordinated series of nouns ('heaven(s), earth, time') that serve as the direct objects; the clause closes with the past tense verb 'آفرید' ('created'), a third‑person past marker typical in Ferdowsi's poetic style.
segments.s468-g90: This repeats the verb 'آفرید' with a new pair of coordinated nouns 'توانایی و ناتوان' (the able/capable and the unable/incapable). The verb is understood to refer back to the same subject in the previous clause ('he created ... and created ...').
segments.s468-g91: 'نگه کن' is the imperative 'look!' formed by the noun 'نگه' (gaze/watch) plus the imperative auxiliary 'کن'. The preposition 'به' introduces the objects 'فرزند و پیوند من' ('my child/descendant and my bond/kin'), with the possessor 'من' following the noun as is normal in Persian.
segments.s468-g92: Another prepositional phrase 'به پوشیدگان خردمند من' — 'به' + noun phrase. 'پوشیدگان' (the covered/veiled/hidden ones) is modified by the adjective 'خردمند' (wise), and the possessor 'من' attaches after the noun phrase, giving 'my veiled wise ones'.
segments.s468-g93: ز is the poetic short form of از ('from/of') and with من ('me') marks the source: 'from me' (i.e. 'ask of me'). پاک‌دل دختر من is a noun phrase 'my pure‑hearted daughter' with the adjective پاک‌دل preceding the noun; the second من is the possessive. بخواه is the 2nd‑person singular imperative of خواستن ('to ask/ask for') and takes the preceding phrase as its object. The word order is inverted for poetic effect: source + object + imperative verb = 'ask of me [for] my pure‑hearted daughter.'
segments.s468-g94: بدارش is verb + enclitic pronoun: an imperative form with the enclitic ‑ش = 'her' (i.e. 'take/bring her'); here it functions as a command to bring or present the woman. به آرام is an adverbial prepositional phrase 'in a calm/gentle manner' (به + آرام used adverbially). بر + پیشگاه means 'toward/to the presence' (پیشگاه = the royal presence / court); here بر has the sense 'toward/to'. Together: 'bring her gently before the presence.'
segments.s468-g95: کجا here functions as a narrative/relational 'where/whereupon' introducing what follows. مادرش is مادر + enclitic ش = 'his mother.' روشنک نام کرد combines the noun روشنک (a female name) with نام کردن ('to name'); کرد is the past tense of کردن used with نام to mean 'called/named (her) Roshanak.' Word order places the given name before the verbal phrase, typical in Persian.
segments.s468-g96: جهان را uses the definite object marker را to mark جهان ('the world') as the object of شاد کردن ('to make happy'); بدو is the dative 'to him/for him.' شاد here is an adjective used with کردن (کرد) to mean 'made (someone) happy.' The conjunction و links the clause to پدرام کرد: here پدرام (a proper name) + کرد is a verbless idiom where کردن supplies the action 'made/called (him) Pedram' — i.e. 'and called him Pedram.' Thus: 'made the world joyful for him, and called him Pedram.'
segments.s468-g105: نگه دارد is a two‑part verbal idea: نگه (a noun/nominal element 'keeping, watch') with دارد 'has/holds' functioning as the main verb — together 'keeps/maintains'. این فال جشن سده is the direct object phrase 'this omen (فال) of the Sadeh festival (جشن سده)'; word order is [verb] [object] (poetic but normal in Persian poetry).
segments.s468-g106: همان here means 'that same / the very same' and introduces what is being preserved: فر 'divine/royal glory' followed by a coordination نوروز و آتشکده 'Nowruz and the fire‑temple'. The phrase is a nominal apposition to the object in the previous clause.
segments.s468-g107: This is a triad of agents listed in nominative position: همان اورمزد و مه و روز مهر — 'that Ahura Mazda and the moon and the day (of) Mithra'. همان points back to the previously mentioned glory; مه here is 'the moon' and روز مهر is a calendrical/theological phrase 'the day of Mehr/Mithra'.
segments.s468-g108: بشوید is an optative/subjunctive form used here as a jussive third‑person plural/collective 'may ... wash' or 'let ... wash' (poetic usage). به introduces the instrument/medium 'into/with', so به آب خرد 'in/with the water of wisdom' and جان و چهر are the direct objects 'soul and face' (ordered: inner then outer).
segments.s468-g109: کند is the simple verbal 'does/makes' (third‑person), taking تازه آیین لهراسپی 'makes (it) the new ritual of Lohrasp' as its object: تازه modifies آیین (new ritual), and لهراسپی is a genitive/attributive form 'of Lohrasp'.
segments.s468-g110: بماند is a present tense/optative of ماندن 'remain' meaning 'remains/shall remain' here; it governs the following nominal predicate کیی دین گشتاسپی 'the Kayanian religion of Gushtasp' where کیی (Kayanian) qualifies دین (religion) and گشتاسپی marks possession 'of Gushtasp'.
segments.s468-g111: مهان را: مهان 'guests' is marked by را as definite/direct object/topic. به مه دارد is an idiomatic expression where به + مه conveys 'in/with greatness/esteem' (مه here used as 'greatness, splendour'); دارد 'has/holds' means 'holds (them)'. The following و که به که is an idiomatic comparative/exclamatory pairing 'and who (compared) to whom' — setting up the rhetorical question in the next segment.
segments.s468-g112: بود is the copula 'was/there was' used to introduce the predicate دین فروزنده و روزبه — دین فروزنده 'the faith‑illuminator' (an epithet/title) and روزبه the proper name Rouzbeh; the line rhetorically asks which people other than these shone as bringers/brighteners of the religion.
segments.s468-g41: یکی آنک گفتی که ایران تراست = 'One thing that you said is that Iran is yours.' یکی here means 'one (thing)' or 'a certain (thing)' and heads the clause. آنک is an archaic/poetic relative meaning 'that which' (≈ 'that'). گفتی is the 2nd-person past form 'you said' in classical Persian (here used as the verb introducing reported content); the provided lemma senses do not list the verbal sense, so the verb sense is marked separately. که introduces the reported content ('that'), ایران is the noun 'Iran (land)' and تراست is a poetic predicative meaning 'is yours' (possessive copula; literally 'it-is-yours').
segments.s468-g42: سر تاج و تخت دلیران تراست = 'The head, crown, and throne of the brave are yours.' This is a nominal list (سر, تاج, تخت) coordinated with و ('and') followed by the genitive modifier دلیران ('of the brave/warriors'). تراست again is the poetic copula 'is yours' assigning possession to the addressee; word order is typical Persian: list of possessed nouns + possessor predicate.
segments.s468-g43: به من مرگ نزدیک‌تر زانک تخت = 'To me, death is nearer than the throne, because...' The prepositional phrase به من ('to me') marks the person affected; the comparative adjective نزدیک‌تر ('nearer') follows the noun مرگ ('death') in typical Persian order (noun + comparative). زانک is an archaic conjunction meaning 'because / since' and introduces the causal clause that follows.
segments.s468-g44: بپردخت تخت و نگون گشت بخت = '(When) he/she/one attained/paid the throne, the throne and (his) fortune became overturned.' بپردخت is an archaic/poetic past 3rd-person form (with the b- prefix) from پرداختن; in context it means 'attained/received/paid for' the throne. تخت is 'throne'; و links the next clause; نگون is an adjective meaning 'overthrown/ill-fated', and گشت is the past of گشتن 'became/turned', so گشت بخت = 'fortune became (overthrown)'. Note: some lexical senses supplied are limited, so the precise verbal nuance is conveyed by context and older usage.
segments.s468-g45: برین است فرجام چرخ بلند = 'From this is the outcome/end of the high wheel.' برین is a fused/poetic form (بر + این) meaning 'on/with this' or 'from this'; است is the copula 'is'; فرجام 'end/outcome' is the subject; چرخ بلند 'the high/lofty wheel' (چرخ here evokes the wheel of fortune) is the noun phrase describing whose outcome it is. Word order is somewhat elevated/poetic but follows Persian nominal construction.
segments.s468-g46: خرامش سوی رنج و سودش گزند = 'Its strutting goes toward suffering, and its profit/gain is harm.' خرامش is a deverbal/nominal form 'its strutting/prancing' (the -ش marks the genitive/possessive 'its'); سوی 'toward(s)' marks direction; رنج 'suffering' is the goal; و connects the clauses; سودش 'its gain/profit' (again -ش = 'its') is followed by گزند 'harm' in a verbless predication (typical in Persian: X گزند = 'X is harm/causes harm').
segments.s468-g47: به من در نگر تا نگویی که من = 'Look at me closely so that you will not say that I...' به من ('to me' / 'at me') plus در نگر is an imperative instruction: در here intensifies/frames the act of looking ('look into/closely'), and نگر is the imperative 'look!' تا introduces a purpose/result ('so that'), while نگویی is the negative-subjunctive/negative present 2nd-person form 'you not say' (classical usage: ن + ماضی/مضارع-subjunctive), and که begins the subordinate clause. The supplied lexical senses for نگویی do not include the verbal form, so that sense is indicated separately.
segments.s468-g48: فزونم ازین نامدار انجمن = 'I am greater/surpass this famed company.' فزونم is the first-person predicate 'I am more / I exceed' (the -م is the 1sg copular/clitic), از indicates comparison 'than / from', این (written as ین) = 'this', نامدار 'renowned/famed' modifies انجمن 'assembly/company/group'. The phrase expresses a comparative claim; the morphological clitic -م on فزونم marks the subject 'I'.
segments.s468-g113: سکندر (subject) + چنین (adverb) + داد (past tense verb) + پاسخ (object) + بدوی (adjective). The verb داد is simple past active 'gave' and governs the object پاسخ; چنین (= 'thus') modifies the manner of the giving. بدوی is a poetic adjective postposed to پاسخ, producing 'an original/primitive reply' or more naturally here 'this reply.'
segments.s468-g114: که‌ای is a vocative/exclamatory particle ('O' in classical Persian) introducing the direct address. نیکدل (adjective) and راست‌گوی (adjective) frame خسرو (noun, vocative 'king') to produce the address 'O kind‑hearted, truthful king.' Word order is poetic; the vocative marker precedes the epithets and name.
segments.s468-g115: پذیرفتم is first‑person past (used here to mean 'I accept / I have accepted'); it is transitive and takes the following noun phrase as object. این پند و اندرز تو is a coordinated definite object ('this advice and counsel of yours'). The object marker تو marks possession ('of you').
segments.s468-g116: فزون here conveys the idea 'more/excessive' and modifies the copula/verb phrase that follows. زین ... برین is a compressed poetic form (برین = بر + این) meaning 'beyond/over this'; نباشم is negative first‑person subjunctive/optative 'may I not be / I will not be', expressing the speaker's resolution. مرز تو 'your limit/boundary' is the locus: together the clause means 'I shall not exceed these limits of yours.' Note the archaic contraction برین (instead of بر این) and the optative flavor of نباشم.
segments.s468-g117: همه نیکویها is the plural definite phrase 'all the good/virtuous things/deeds.' به جای آورم uses the idiom به جای آوردن ('to put in place' = 'to perform, accomplish'); آورم is first‑person form ('I will bring/do'). The verb phrase thus expresses the speaker's resolve to carry out all good deeds.
segments.s468-g118: خرد را is the definite object 'wisdom' marked by را. بدین رهنمای is a prepositional/agentive phrase 'by this guidance' or 'according to this guide' (بدین = ب + دین in the text, a compressed classical form meaning 'by/with this'); آورم is again 'I will bring/bring about.' Together the clause says the speaker will align or entrust wisdom to this guidance. (Note: بدین is a poetic fused form equivalent to 'به این'.)
segments.s468-g119: جهاندار is the subject 'the world‑lord/sovereign.' دست سکندر is a possessive sequence 'the hand of Alexander' (poetic word order with the possessed noun before the possessor). گرفت is past tense 'took/seized.' The clause is straightforward S‑O‑V: 'The sovereign took Alexander's hand.'
segments.s468-g120: به زاری is an adverbial phrase 'in lamentation' (manner). خروشیدن is the verbal noun/infinitive 'to cry out' or 'to wail.' اندر گرفت is a classical periphrastic construction meaning 'began to' (اندر + گرفت), so together خروشیدن اندر گرفت = 'he began to cry aloud / took to wailing.' The clause thus means that, lamenting, he broke into a cry.
segments.s468-g73: سکندر is the subject (the proper name 'Alexander'). ز دیده is an ablative/locative phrase 'from the eyes' (poetic z = از). ببارید is a past‑tense verb ('rained/ fell in drops') agreeing with the singular subject. خون here names what fell; the phrase literally means 'from the eyes blood fell' — i.e. he shed blood from his eyes.
segments.s468-g74: بران is a contracted poetic بر آن 'on that/thereupon', linking this clause to the previous action. شاه خسته is noun + adjective (king, weary). به خاک اندرون is a prepositional phrase 'to/onto the ground inside' (به + خاک + اندرون); the verb of falling is understood from the context (the clause is a compressed narrative: 'thereupon the weary king fell to the ground').
segments.s468-g75: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. دارا here is the personal name (Dâra); بدید is the past tense 'saw' with آن as its object ('saw that'). ز دل‌درد او is an ablative/causal phrase 'from his heart‑ache' (دل‌درد = sorrow/heartache) describing the cause of what follows.
segments.s468-g76: This is a nominal/paratactic sequence with an implied verb: روان اشک خونین = 'flowing, blood‑stained tears' (روان as an adjective/participle 'flowing'); رخ زرد او = 'his face (was) pale' (رخ + adjective زرد, with او marking possession). Persian poetry often omits the copula, so these two noun+adjective phrases describe his visible condition.
segments.s468-g77: بدو گفت is 'he said to him' (بدو = to him, گفت = said). مگری is a prohibitive/negative admonition here (literally 'do not/ do not suppose'), introducing the warning clause; کزین ('که از این' contracted) = 'that/from this', and سود نیست = 'there is no benefit' (سود = benefit, نیست = is not). The clause is quoted speech compressed in poetic style.
segments.s468-g78: از آتش is an ablative 'from the fire'. مرا را here functions as a dative/possessive ('for me' / 'my'), with را marking the phrase as a focused/theme phrase. بهره = 'share/portion'; جز دود نیست = 'there is nothing except smoke' (جز = except, دود = smoke, نیست = is not). The whole line: 'as for fire, my portion is nothing but smoke.'
segments.s468-g79: چنین بود is 'thus was' (چنین = thus, بود = was, past copula). بخشش = 'gift/portion' (or 'grant'); ز بخشنده‌ام = 'from my Giver' — بخشنده‌ام is بخشنده + possessive clitic ‑ام meaning 'my giver' (a poetic/genitive construction). The line states that this is the kind of gift received from the Giver.
segments.s468-g80: هم = 'also/too'. از روزگار = 'by/from fate/time' (روزگار here = fortune/fate). درخشنده‌ام is an adjectival predicate 'I am resplendent/illustrious' with the first‑person clitic attached (-ام = 'I am'); poetically it asserts the speaker's illustrious standing due to the times/fate.
segments.s468-g9: برفتند is a 3rd-person plural past verb (‘they went/left’) placed at the clause start by poetic inversion; هر دو ('each/both') is the subject phrase that follows the verb. به پیش اندرون is a sequence of prepositional/directional elements: به (towards) + پیش (forward/in front) + اندرون (the interior), giving the direction 'toward the inner quarters.'
segments.s468-g10: دل و جان رومی is a coordinated possessive phrase 'the Roman's heart and soul' (two nouns linked, followed by the modifier رومی). پر از خشم و خون is a nominal predicate: پر (adjectival 'full') + از (introducing contents) + خشم و خون ('anger and blood/bloodshed'), so 'full of anger and blood,' with no copula needed in Persian.
segments.s468-g11: چو + adjective + شد is a temporal clause 'when he became/grew near' (چو = 'when/as', نزدیک شد = 'came near'). روی دارا بدید places the object روی ('face') and its possessor/modifier دارا (here functioning as the name/possessor) before the verb بدید ('he saw'), an archaic past form of دیدن; the verb at the end is typical Persian sequencing.
segments.s468-g12: پر از خون is again the adjectival predicate 'full of blood' (پر + از + خون). The phrase بر و روی gives the location 'on brow and face' (بر = on/upon, و = and, روی = face). چون شنبلید is a simile 'like a fenugreek ear/spike' (چون = like/as + شنبلید = fenugreek spike), completing the description of appearance.
segments.s468-g13: بفرمود is the honorific past 'he commanded/ordered.' تا introduces a subordinate clause of purpose/result here ('that/so that'), and راه نگذاشتند is the reported clause where نگذاشتند is the past 3rd-person plural negative form of گذاشتن meaning 'they did not allow' (i.e. 'they did not permit passage'). The sequence expresses: 'He ordered that they allow no way/passage.'
segments.s468-g14: دستور او را is the object phrase 'his order' with را marking it as definite/accusative. نگه داشتند is the compound verb 'they kept/held' (نگه + داشتند), literally 'kept watch/kept'—here it means they obeyed or maintained his command; the verb is past 3rd plural.
segments.s468-g15: سکندر is the subject 'Alexander.' ز + bاره + درآمد is a prepositional/verb phrase meaning 'came out/from the bout/charge' (ز = from; here باره = an episode/charge; درآمد = 'entered/came out' used as a dynamic verb). چو باد is a simile 'as/like the wind' (چو = as/when, باد = wind), producing 'Alexander came out of the mêlée like the wind.'
segments.s468-g16: سر مرد خسته is the direct object 'the tired man's head' (سر = head, مرد خسته = the weary man). به ران is a prepositional phrase 'onto (his) thigh' (به = to/onto, ران = thigh). بر نهاد is the past verb 'placed' (بر + نهاد, with نهاد here the past of نهادن 'to put'); typical Persian order places object and prepositional phrase before the verb.
segments.s468-g25: تو (explicit second-person pronoun) adds emphasis to the imperative. برخیز is a second-person imperative/command 'rise!' and is followed by و coordinating the next imperative. بر + مهد زرین is a prepositional phrase ('upon the golden cradle/seat') where مهد (noun) takes the adjective زرین after it; نشین is the second-person imperative 'sit'. The overall order (Verb — preposition + noun — verb) is poetic but standard: commands joined by و and target placed between preposition and verb.
segments.s468-g26: وگر introduces a conditional ('and/ but if'); هست is the copula/existential 'there is' used parenthetically. نیروت is the noun نیرو 'strength' with the 2nd-person suffix (-ت) so it means 'your strength'. بر زین نشین repeats the preposition + noun + imperative pattern ('sit on the saddle'). In effect the clause reads 'and if there is your strength, sit on the saddle' — هست + noun phrase sets the condition and the final نشین is the imperative result.
segments.s468-g27: ز (...) آورم is a simple verbal clause meaning 'I will bring from ...'. The preposition ز (poetic for 'from'/'out of') is used twice for parallelism: ز هند و ز رومت = 'from India and from Rome'. پزشک آورم is first-person present/future sense ('I bring' / 'I will bring'); in classical Persian the present form can indicate intended future action.
segments.s468-g28: ز here means 'because of' / 'from' (source) introducing the cause 'درد تو' ('your pain'). خونین is an adjective 'blood-red / bloodstained' modifying سرشک ('tear, teardrop'), and آورم again is 'I will bring'. The word order places the causal phrase first (ز درد تو) and ends with the verb آورم, a common poetic sequence: 'from your pain I will bring bloody teardrops.'
segments.s468-g29: سپارم is a first-person verb 'I shall entrust / I will entrust'. The clitic ترا (broken out here as ت + را) is the object 'to you'/'you' and marks the person who receives the entrustment; را is the definite/object marker attached in the clitic cluster. پادشاهی و تخت ('kingship and throne') are the things being entrusted. Persian often places the recipient pronoun close to the verb in poetry, hence سپارم ترا پادشاهی و تخت.
segments.s468-g30: چو here means 'when' (temporal). بهتر شوی is a dependent clause 'you become better' with شوی (present/subjunctive of شدن) indicating change of state. ما is the subject 'we', and ببندیم رخت ('we will bind/pack the baggage') uses the verb ببندیم (first-person plural subjunctive/imperative-habitual used as future here) with رخت meaning 'baggage/things'. The clause thus expresses a future action contingent on recovery: 'when you recover, we will pack up.'
segments.s468-g31: جفا پیشگان is a compound noun phrase 'the cruelty-doers / the cruel ones'. ترا is the clitic object pronoun 'you' and in poetry can follow the addressed noun for emphasis (vocative/emphatic placement); هم کنون means 'even now / right now'. Grammatically this segment sets up the addressee and time: 'O cruel ones, you (together) right now ...' anticipating the verb in the next segment.
segments.s468-g32: بیاویزم is first-person (optative/intentional) 'I will hang' / 'I shall hang', here expressing the speaker's imminent action. از دارشان is a prepositional phrase 'from their dar' where دار is the poetic word for 'gallows' (دارشان = their gallows). سرنگون is a resultative adjective 'overthrown / cast down' describing the fate inflicted. The verb + از‑phrase + adjective sequence yields 'I will hang you and throw you down from their gallows.'
segments.s468-g33: چنانچون ... شنیدیم دوش = a clause meaning “as we heard ... last night.” چنانچون is a subordinating adverb ‘as/just as’; ز + پیران is the poetic ‘from the elders’; شنیدیم is the 1st‑person plural past ‘we heard’; دوش (=“last night”) is adverbial and placed after the verb (a common archaic/poetic order).
segments.s468-g34: دلم گشت پر خون و جان پر ز جوش = two coordinated result phrases. دلم is ‘my heart’ (دل + possessive suffix -م); گشت is past ‘became’ (turned into); پر here is adjectival ‘full (of)’ and takes the following noun (پر خون = ‘full of blood’). و links the second phrase; جان پر ز جوش literally ‘soul/life full from/with ardor’ (ز introduces the source/reason).
segments.s468-g35: ز یک‌شاخ و یک بیخ و پیراهنیم = a clause stating common origin/identity. ز = ‘from/of’; یک‌شاخ and یک بیخ are parallel nominal phrases (‘one‑horned’ / ‘one root’ used metaphorically); پیراهنیم is پیراهن + the 1st‑pl. copular/identifying suffix -یم meaning ‘we are (of) the same garment’ — a poetic way to say ‘we belong to the same stock/covering.’
segments.s468-g36: به بیشی چرا تخمه را برکنیم = a rhetorical question. به بیشی = ‘for the sake of more/for a little extra’ (به = ‘for/to’ + بیشی = ‘more’); چرا = ‘why’; تخمه را = definite object ‘the seed/offspring’ (را marks the object); برکنیم is 1st‑pl. subjunctive/optative ‘(should) we uproot/remove’ — here used rhetorically (‘why should we uproot…’).
segments.s468-g37: چو بشنید دارا به آواز گفت = temporal clause + reporting. چو = ‘when’; بشنید is past ‘he heard’ (here with چو = ‘when he heard’); دارا is the person’s name (Dara) — listed senses do not include the proper‑name use; به آواز = adverbial phrase ‘in a voice/ aloud’ (به + noun); گفت = ‘said/spoke’ (here used as past verb; the provided senses list does not match this verb form).
segments.s468-g38: که همواره با تو خرد باد جفت = content clause introduced by که ‘that.’ همواره = ‘always’; با تو = ‘with you’; خرد = ‘wisdom’ (noun); باد is the optative verb ‘may (it be)’ (a fixed poetic jussive); جفت = ‘pair/companion’ or ‘mate,’ so the phrase means ‘May wisdom always be your companion.’ Word order is inverted for the optative: noun + باد + complement.
segments.s468-g39: برآنم که از پاک دادار خویش = برآنم = ‘I am resolved / I intend’ (stative verb + 1st‑sg. suffix); که introduces the subordinate clause; از پاک دادار = ‘from the pure Creator’ (از = ‘from/of’; پاک دادار = ‘the pure Giver/Creator’); خویش (here split as خوی + ش) is a reflexive/possessive element meaning ‘one’s own’ or ‘his/your own’ (context: ‘from the pure Creator himself/your own Creator’).
segments.s468-g40: بیابی تو پاداش گفتار خویش = main idea of the subordinate clause. بیابی is subjunctive/optative form ‘may you find / you should find’ (here used jussively/wishing); تو is the explicit subject ‘you’ (poetic emphasis); پاداش گفتار خویش = ‘the reward of your speech/words’ (گفتار = ‘speech’; خویش + ش = ‘your own’). The clause expresses hope/wish that the addressee receive divine reward.
segments.s468-g17: نگه کرد is a past tense verb phrase 'he looked/kept watch.' تا introduces a temporal subordinate clause meaning 'until.' The clause خسته گوینده هست is a predicative noun phrase with adjective before noun (poetic inversion) followed by the copula هست ('is/was'); together they mean 'the speaker (being) tired.' The structure therefore reads: 'He watched until the (already) tired speaker was (so).'
segments.s468-g18: بمالید is a past action 'he rubbed' (3rd person past). The prepositional phrase بر چهر او marks the target 'upon his face' (بر = on; چهر = face; او = him/his). هر دو دست is a manner/instrument phrase 'with both hands' (هر دو = both, دست = hands). Word order places the verb first, then the target and the instrumental phrase.
segments.s468-g19: ز + سر is a prepositional phrase 'from the head.' برگرفت is a past tense verb 'took up / removed.' افسر خسرویش is a genitive/compound noun 'his royal helmet' (خسرویش = 'kingly/his kingly'). Syntactically: 'From his head he removed the royal helmet.'
segments.s468-g20: گشاد here functions as the past/resultative sense of 'open/loosen' (he loosened/opened). آن is a demonstrative 'that' (referring back to the helmet), and the coordinating و links جوشن پهلویش 'the cuirass at his side' as a second object. پهلویش is lokative/possessive 'at his side / his side.' Overall: 'he loosened that (the helmet) and the side-cuirass.'
segments.s468-g21: ز دیده is a source phrase 'from the eye.' ببارید is an intransitive past verb 'fell / came down' (used of drops/tears). چندی is a temporal adverb 'for a while,' and سرشک is the noun 'a teardrop / drops of tears' placed after the verb by poetic inversion. The clause means 'teardrops fell from the eye for a while.'
segments.s468-g22: تن خسته is the noun phrase 'the weary/tired body' (adjective after noun). را is the definite/direct-object marker marking that phrase. دور functions adverbially 'from afar / at a distance' modifying دید (past 'saw'). The postverbal phrase از پزشک places the agent/actor after the verb (poetic inversion): 'the physician saw the weary body from a distance.'
segments.s468-g23: بدو is a prepositional phrase 'to him.' گفت is the past 'said' introducing reported speech. The subordinate clause کین بر تو آسان شود uses کین ('hatred/vengeance') as the subject/theme, بر تو 'upon you' as the logical target, and آسان شود 'may be made easy' — شود here conveys optative/wish ('may it become'). The whole clause is an optative blessing/wish: 'May this hatred be light/easy for you.'
segments.s468-g24: دل is 'heart'; بدسگالت is an adjectival/compound form 'ill‑omened / ill‑fated' modifying دل. هراسان شود is again an optative 'may (it) become frightened / be filled with fear' (شود marking 'may it become'). Grammatically this is a parallel wish: 'May your ill‑omened heart be frightened.'
segments.s468-g1: به نزدیک + name is a prepositional/adverbial phrase meaning 'near/toward Alexander' (به + نزدیک). آمد is the simple past 3rd‑person singular 'came'. وزیر, the agent, follows the verb here — an inversion common in poetic/classical Persian (verb before subject) so the whole segment reads 'the vizier came near Alexander.'
segments.s468-g2: که‌ای is an archaic/poetic vocative (roughly 'O'), introducing direct address. شاه is the noun 'king' and the following words are epithets: پیروز 'victorious' and دانش‌پذیر 'teachable/receptive to knowledge'; و links the two adjectives. Together it forms the vocative: 'O victorious and teachable king.'
segments.s468-g3: بکشتیم is the past tense 1st‑person plural 'we killed' (perfective). دشمنت is the direct object 'your enemy' and را is the definite/object marker attached after it. ناگهان is an adverb 'suddenly' placed after the predicate for emphasis: 'we killed your enemy suddenly.'
segments.s468-g4: سرآمد here functions adverbially/parenthetically as 'at last' or 'finally' (poetic use). برو is the imperative 'go'. تاج و تخت مهان is the direct object phrase 'the crown and throne of the mehān' (i.e. the great/nobles); مهان is a descriptive genitive following تخت. The order (adverb → imperative → object) is typical of classical style.
segments.s468-g5: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. بشنید is the past 3rd‑person singular 'he heard'. گفتار 'speech/words' precedes the proper name جانوشیار (poetic ordering without the modern ezāfe), giving 'when he heard Jānūshyār's speech.'
segments.s468-g6: سکندر is the subject 'Alexander'. چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' modifying the verb. گفت here is the past tense 'said/spoke' (classical narrative). با + ماهیار marks the addressee 'to/with Māhyār' — in classical Persian با can introduce the person spoken to. The whole segment: 'Alexander thus said to Māhyār.'
segments.s468-g7: This is a double relative/interrogative construction. The first که introduces a relative/interrogative 'which' (که دشمن 'which enemy'), the second که introduces the subordinate clause 'that you cast down' (افگندی, 2nd‑person past 'you threw/cast down'). اکنون 'now' and کجا 'where' form the question; ست is the copula 'is' placed at the end (poetic word order): 'Which enemy that you cast down — where is (he) now?'
segments.s468-g8: بباید is a modal/impersonal form 'it is necessary / you must'. نمودن is the infinitive 'to show'. به من is the indirect object 'to me'. راه راست 'the straight/true way' is the direct object; classical Persian often places the modal + infinitive before objects, so this reads 'You must show me the straight/right way.'
segments.s468-g57: همان نیز is a topic/demonstrative plus adverb: 'that same / likewise'. It front‑projects the item being spoken of. فرزند و پیوستگان is a nominative noun phrase in apposition ('sons and followers') with no verb in this clause (elliptical continuation).
segments.s468-g58: چه here is an exclamative 'what/how (a...)' introducing an emphatic description. پیوستگان is the head noun ('followers/retainers'), while the following nouns form epithets: داغ دل is a compound 'burning‑grief of the heart' (heart‑sore), and خستگان is an adjectival/nominative plural 'the weary' describing them.
segments.s468-g59: زمان و زمین forms a compound subject 'time and the world/earth' (used metonymically for circumstances). بنده is a humble self‑reference meaning 'me/I' (classical usage), and بد پیش من is an adjectival/prepositional phrase 'ill‑disposed toward me' (پیش من = 'toward/in relation to me'). The clause is elliptical for 'were' and predicates that circumstances treated the speaker badly.
segments.s468-g60: چنین بود is a simple past copular phrase 'it was thus'. تا introduces the temporal limit 'until'. بخت بد خویش من = 'my bad fortune' with بخت (fate/fortune) modified by بد, and خویش (here expressed in two parts as خوی + ش + من) functioning as the possessive 'one's own / my', so the phrase means 'until my own ill fate'.
segments.s468-g61: ز نیکی = 'from/away from goodness' (ز = 'from/because of'). جدا مانده‌ام is a perfect/resultant construction 'I have remained apart / I have been cut off' (مانده‌ام = 'I remain/was left'). زین نشان is instrumental/causal 'by this sign/token' (زین as bound prepositional/instrumental or 'this' + نشان 'sign/token'), marking the reason for the separation.
segments.s468-g62: گرفتار is a predicative adjective/participle 'trapped' or 'in bondage'. در دست is an idiom meaning 'in the hand/power of' (در = 'in', دست = 'hand/power'), and مردم‌کشان is a descriptive plural 'those who slay people / people‑killers'—the agent controlling the speaker.
segments.s468-g63: ز introduces the source/point ('of/with respect to'), so ز فرزند و خویشان = 'as for/with regard to children and relatives'. شده ناامید is a verbal/adjectival phrase 'have become hopeless' (شده = 'become', ناامید = 'hopeless'), with the subject implied from prior lines (the speaker).
segments.s468-g64: سیه شد جهان is a simple clause 'the world became dark' (سیه = 'black/dark', شد = 'became'). The second half 'و دو دیده سپید' omits the verb (ellipsis of شد) and reads 'and (my) two eyes (turned) white' — سپید as predicate adjective ('pale/white'). The juxtaposition is poetic: world darkened, eyes became white/pale.
